content.problemStatement:
Mario was going to gift Princess Peach a string SSS of **even** length NNN.

Mario was clumsy and thus, broke the string in the middle. He now has two strings AAA and BBB such that A=S[1,N2]A = S[1, \frac{N}{2}]A=S[1,2N​] and B=S[N2+1,N]B = S[\frac{N}{2} + 1, N]B=S[2N​+1,N].

Mario is not sure about the order in which he should join the strings AAA and BBB to get the string SSS. Thus, he joins the strings in any random order. Find whether it is guaranteed that Mario will get the same string SSS if he joins the strings AAA and BBB in any order.

Note that S[L,R]S[L, R]S[L,R] denotes a substring of string SSS starting at index LLL and having a length (R−L+1)(R - L + 1)(R−L+1).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input:

The first line of each test case contains NNN - the length of the initial string SSS.
The second line contains the string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, print `YES` if it is guaranteed that Mario will get the same string SSS irrespective of the order in which he joins the strings AAA and BBB and `NO` otherwise.


You may print each character of the string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings `YES`, `yEs`, `yes`, and `yeS` will all be treated as identical).
constraints: S consists only of lowercase English letters
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an even integer N (the length of the original string). The second line contains a string S of length N consisting of lowercase English letters.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing "YES" if the first half of S is identical to the second half (i.e., the string is guaranteed to be the same regardless of the order of concatenation), otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
For each test case we compare the two halves:
1) abcabc -> "abc" vs "abc" => equal => YES.
2) abcdef -> "abc" vs "def" => not equal => NO.
3) aaaa   -> "aa" vs "aa" => equal => YES.
4) baab   -> "ba" vs "ab" => not equal => NO.

solution.approach: For each test case, split the string S into two halves: A = S[0 .. N/2-1] and B = S[N/2 .. N-1]. The concatenations AB and BA are equal if and only if A == B. Therefore, simply compare the two substrings. If they match, print YES; otherwise, print NO. The operation is O(N) per test case, which easily satisfies the constraints.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string in that test case. Overall O(T * N_max) ≤ 3·10^6 operations.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input string.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().split()
    it = iter(data)
    t = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = int(next(it))
        s = next(it)
        half = n // 2
        out.append('YES' if s[:half] == s[half:] else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
